1.0 POLICY SECTION 
The work of the Burnaby-New Westminster Task Force on Sexually Exploited and At-
Risk Youth (Task Force) is aligned with the following Council-adopted policies, plans 
and strategies: 
 
• Social Sustainability Strategy (2011); 
• Equity Policy (1994, updated 2020); and 
• Community Safety and Well-Being Plan (2025). 
2.0 BACKGROUND 
Since 1998, the Task Force has been working to address the sexual abuse of children 
and youth in sex trade. The membership is a multi-sectoral coalition, with staff 
representation from the City of Burnaby, City of New Westminster, Burnaby and New 
Westminster School Districts, Burnaby Public Library, Burnaby RCMP, New Westminster 
Police, Fraser Health Authority, the Ministry of Children and Family Development, and 
several non-profit agencies who provide services to youth. The Task Force operates as 
a sub-committee of the Public Safety Committee and is chaired by Councillor Santiago 
and vice-chaired by Burnaby School Board Trustee Hayes. From New Westminster, 
Councillor Henderson represents the City of New Westminster and Trustee Smith 
represents the New Westminster School District.  
Over the years, the work of the Task Force has focused on two priorities: 
1) To ensure provision of adequate support, services and treatment for sexually 
exploited children and youth; and  

Subject: SU: Burnaby-New Westminster Task Force on  
Sexually Exploited and At-Risk Youth (2025) 
PSC Meeting February 26, 2026.................................................. Page 2 
 
2) To prevent the commercial sexual exploitation of children and youth. 
3.0 GENERAL INFORMATION 
The Committee and Council were last updated on the work of the Task Force in June 
2022. This report provides an overview of the Task Force’s key activities since that time 
and outlines the Task force’s priorities for its work plan. City staff are involved in the 
Task Force as follows: Social Planning provides in-kind administrative support and 
Community Safety aligns Task Force work with the Community Safety and Well-Being 
Plan. The key activities of the Task Force are summarized below. 
3.1 Information Sharing and Networking 
The Task Force presents an opportunity for service providers to share information and 
trends (e.g. recruitment activities, age of recruitment, geographic instances of 
recruitment etc.). These opportunities are crucial to the prevention of sexual exploitation 
and the identification of intervention methods/activities to support at-risk youth. The Task 
Force provides a unique, focussed opportunity for the sharing of information across 
jurisdictions (Burnaby-New Westminster) by a range of social service providers, School 
Districts, police forces (New Westminster Police, Burnaby RCMP), and local 
government representatives.  
The long-standing nature of the Task Force has also nurtured the establishment of 
effective working relationships among these same bodies, which has increased 
membership interest, and the efficiency and timeliness of local response. The Task 
Force remains an important vehicle for these relationships and for the transmissions of 
knowledge as new agency representatives/workers are welcomed into the community.  
Information sharing and networking was supported by: 
• Quarterly meetings (a mix of in-person and online) to share trends, connections 
and discussion topics on youth exploitation in Burnaby and New Westminster. 
• A networking event at Tommy Douglas Library. 
3.2 Professional Development and Service Provider Capacity Building  
The Task Force have renewed its efforts to curate related professional development 
opportunities and educational sessions. Over the years, the Task Force received 
presentations on Building Safer Communities initiatives from the City of Burnaby and 
New Westminster, as well as an anti-trafficking training session from the Covenant 
House. 
On November 7, 2025, the City, in partnership with Burnaby School District, hosted a 
professional development day for service providers in Burnaby and New Westminster. 
The event, “Combatting Online Sexual Exploitation: Tools for Frontline Professionals” 
had informational sessions provided by speakers from PLEA Community Services and 
from the Burnaby RCMP Child Abuse Sexual Offences (CASO) Unit as well as 
participant networking opportunities.

The Task Force will continue meeting in 2026 and utilize the feedback from the 
professional development event to identify potential future workshops for Task Force 
members. Identifying ways to tie this Task Force to other City-related initiatives, such as 
implementing actions in the Community Safety and Well-Being Plan, will also be 
explored.    
4.0 COMMUNICATION AND COMMUNITY ENGAGEMENT 
Should Council receive this report for information, a copy of this report will be sent out to 
the Social Planning Committee, Burnaby Public Library Board, Burnaby School District, 
City of New Westminster and the New Westminster School District. 
5.0 FINANCIAL CONSIDERATIONS  
Historically, a $2,000 budget for this Task Force had been allocated as part of the 
Boards, Advisory Bodies, and Commissions Budget. Currently, there is no financial 
request to this Task Force as part of the 2026-2030 Financial Plan.  
Respectfully submitted, 
 
Graham De La Gorgendiere, General Manager Community Safety and  
E. W. Kozak, General Manager Planning and Development

briefs.title: Burnaby approves updated Zoning Bylaw, effective July 1, 2026.
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved updates to the Zoning Bylaw. The changes include more flexible rules, easier-to-understand language, and better visuals. The updated bylaw also includes new rules for landscaping, parking, and tenant protection. The bylaw is scheduled to take effect on July 1, 2026.
briefs.action_required: Review how the updated Zoning Bylaw affects your property and future development plans.

key_dates.label: Burnaby updated Zoning Bylaw takes effect

briefs.industries: Construction & Development, Landlord & Property Mgmt, Tenant, Retail, Food Service

briefs.title: Burnaby to Regulate Bear Spray Sales with Business Licence Bylaw Changes.
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council voted to amend the Business Licence Bylaw and the Bylaw Notice Enforcement Bylaw. The changes will regulate the sale of bear spray in Burnaby. This aims to manage public safety.
briefs.action_required: Review the updated Business Licence Bylaw for bear spray sales.

briefs.title: Burnaby approved Solo Karaoke liquor licence at 6462 Kingsway after investigation.
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a liquor primary licence for Solo Karaoke at 102–6462 Kingsway. This allows them to serve liquor from 9:00 a.m. to 2:00 a.m. daily. Minors with adults are allowed until 10:00 p.m. Council made the decision after investigating allegations of unauthorized liquor service and finding no evidence to support them.

briefs.title: Burnaby Council OKs Sending Concerns About Metro 2050 Changes
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved sending a letter to Metro Vancouver expressing concerns about proposed changes to Metro 2050, the regional growth strategy. The changes, requested by Surrey, Langley, and Delta, could expand urban boundaries, reduce protection for rural lands, and lower agreement needed for land-use changes.
briefs.locations: Burnaby, Surrey, Langley, Delta, British Columbia

briefs.title: Burnaby Approved BC Hydro Statutory Right-of-Way at 4111 Norland Ave
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved granting BC Hydro a Statutory Right-of-Way (SRW) at 4111 Norland Avenue. An SRW gives BC Hydro the right to access part of the property to install, operate, and maintain electrical equipment needed for the Community Safety Building.

briefs.title: Burnaby to Apply for UBCM Grant to Improve Development Approvals
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council authorized staff to apply for a grant of up to $200,000 from the Union of BC Municipalities (UBCM). The grant will fund improvements to the City's Development Approvals Process, focusing on faster approvals for housing projects using modern construction methods and standardized designs.

briefs.title: Burnaby approved $580,985 for ServiceNow IT ticketing software licenses until March 2029.
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved a $580,985 contract with ServiceNow, Inc. for IT ticketing software licenses. The licenses support the City's IT ticketing system, which helps staff submit and track IT requests. The contract covers March 16, 2026, to March 15, 2029.

briefs.title: Burnaby Approved Changes to Community Grant Policy and Application Process
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council approved changes to the Community Grant Policy and application form. The changes clarify grant use restrictions, confirm Burnaby-based non-profit requirements, and refine eligibility for Food Security Initiative Grants to focus on ongoing programs. The application form is also updated to remove an unused applicant type and request data on Burnaby residents served.
briefs.action_required: Review the updated Community Grant Policy and application form for the 2026 cycle.

briefs.title: Burnaby Approves Parks, Recreation, and Culture Plan for Future Investment
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council approved the "Making Life Better: Parks, Recreation and Culture Plan." The plan guides future investments and decisions for parks, recreation, and culture services. It aims to ensure these services are resilient, fair, and financially sustainable as Burnaby grows.
briefs.action_required: Review the plan to understand upcoming changes to Burnaby's recreation programs.
briefs.industries: Retail, Food Service, Hotel & Accommodation, Construction & Development

briefs.title: Burnaby to study new parking lots in Burnaby Heights, using reserve funds.
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council authorized using the Off-Street Parking Reserve Fund to study building new parking lots at 4301/4305 and 4171 Pender Street in Burnaby Heights. The city will also start the rezoning process for these properties. The goal is to support local businesses by adding parking spaces.

briefs.title: Burnaby receives $20.1M from Trans Mountain for safety reserve program.
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council received a report on the Burnaby Mountain Safety Reserve Program. The City has an agreement with Trans Mountain Corporation to receive $20.1 million over 21 years. These funds will support emergency planning, safety, infrastructure, and park projects on Burnaby Mountain.

briefs.title: Burnaby to use gaming revenue for approved projects.
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council received an update on the Gaming Reserve, which uses funds from the Grand Villa (Gateway Casino). The city uses these funds for Council-approved projects that protect or improve Burnaby’s environment, public safety, arts, and culture. At the end of 2025, the Gaming Reserve balance was $11,667,088.

briefs.title: Burnaby Receives Update on Civic Projects: Restaurant Expansion, Theatre Renewal, Recreation Centre, and More.
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council received an update on several significant civic projects. These projects include the Mintara Restaurant expansion, James Cowan Theatre renewal, Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre, Community Safety Building, Cameron and Brentwood Community Centres, and Community Facilities Fit-Out Project. All projects remain on budget and on schedule.

key_dates.label: Mintara Restaurant expansion handover to PRC
James Cowan Theatre handover to PRC
Burnaby Lake Recreation Centre handover to PRC
Cameron Community Centre and Library handover to PRC

briefs.industries: Food Service, Construction & Development, Retail, Hotel & Accommodation

briefs.title: Burnaby to Expand Recreation with New Facilities; Staffing Plan Approved
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council received a report about the Project Plan-People-Play. The plan prepares for the opening of three new recreation centres. It focuses on staffing, training, and community engagement to ensure smooth operations.

briefs.title: Burnaby received report on Burnaby-New Westminster Task Force on Sexually Exploited and At-Risk Youth.
briefs.summary: Burnaby Council received a report about the Burnaby-New Westminster Task Force on Sexually Exploited and At-Risk Youth. The task force shares information, offers training, and builds relationships between service providers, school districts, police, and local governments to support at-risk youth.

briefs.title: Burnaby Council received Fire Department status update to December 2025.
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council received the Burnaby Fire Department's status update. The report covers the period from September 2024 to the end of August 2025. It includes statistics and major events.

briefs.title: Burnaby Council received RCMP policing operations and performance report.
briefs.summary: Burnaby City Council received a report about the RCMP's policing work from August to November 2025. The report also included updates on the Annual Performance Plan from July to September 2025. The report covers community engagement and online crime reporting.
